Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-90-774
Dear ur. Jeffries:
This is in response to your request for advice regarding the
conflict of interesl provisions of the Political Reforn Act (the
1116g111.1 Because the advice you seek^is genera], this letter is
in the nature of informal assistance. z
OUESTIONS
1. 
would you need to disqualify yourself fron participation
in a governnental decision on uater levels at Lake Elsinore?
2. 
would you need to disqualify yourself with respect to
decisions of the Lake Elsinore ltanagenent Authority (rrLEljlArr)
regarding LEMA acquisition of parcel's sirnilar to those you own?
CONCLUS IONS
1. 
Your financial interest in property 300 feet frorn the
potential boundary of Lake Elsinore would require you to
disqualify yourseJ,f from decisions in connection with the water
Ievel of Ln! faXe unless there rrould be no financial inpact from
the decision upon you or your property.

ess otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 Californ■

Paqe 2
2. 
You would be required to disqualify yourself fron
participating in LEMA decisions regarding acquisitions of property
wittrin roo feet of your property unless there was no financial
irnpact fron the decision upon you or your property- You would be
required to disqualify yourself fron the acquisition decision if
the acquisition decision included a provision for improvement of
street!, water, storm drai.nage or sirnilar facilities 
which
irnprovernents would improve the services avail-able to your
property .
AdditionaIIy, you would have to disqualify yourself fron the
acquisition deciaio; if the properties to be acquired were located
be€ween 3oo and 2,500 feet of the boundaries of your property and
the decision would have a reasonably foreseeabLe financial effect
of at least $1O,O0o on the fair market value of any of your
properties or an effect on rental value of at least S1,000 per
year on any of your properties.
FACTS
You have recently been elected to the Elsinore valley
Municipal Water District 
('tEvMwDtr) as the Director of Division 3'
In 198-5, Evl.IwD joined with several other agencies in projects to
enhance lake st;bilization 
at Lake Elsinore. These agencies
comprise LEMA. As an EvMwD director, you anticipate participating
in irue decisions, among which nay be decisions to acguire parcels
of lakefront property.
LEMA is involved in budgetary natters as well as natters
pertaining to safety, water replenishnent, use perrnits, flood
Lontrof and several other areai relevant to Lake Elsinore and its
users and visitors. 
EVMWD is the lead agency for the LElitA project
and you anticipate being asked to participate in governmental
decisions relating to LEMA areas of concern.
You are concerned because you have a fifty percent ownership
interest in real property parcels fronting on Lake Elsinore. 
Your
mother is the ownlr of tle renaining fifty 
Percent interest in the
parcels. You believe that you nay be facinq a conflict of
interest if you participate in decisions pertaining to the lake.
ANALYSI S
Section 87100 prohibits any public offj'cia1s frorn making,
partici-pating in, oi usini their official position to infLuence a
governnintal decision in which they know or have reason to know
fhey have a financial interest. 
An official 
has a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision wiIl have a naterial financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the official 
or a
member of his or her inmediate fanily, or on:


our Eile No.
Page 3
I-90-774
Any real property in vhich the public official
has a direct or indirect interest vorth one
thousand dollars (S1,00o) or nore.
Section 87103 (b) .
As a director of a rnunicipal r'rater district, 
you are a public
official. 
(Section a2o4a.) You have an interest in real Property
,oitn .o." than $1,ooo. Accordingly, you are prohibited frorn
participating in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that
Ln. a""i=ion-r,ri11 have a uraterial financial effect, distinquishable
frorn the effect on the public generally, on you or on your real
property interest.
Foreseeab i I itv
The effects of a decision are reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that they !'ill occur' To be
foreseeable, the effects of a decision rnust be nore than a mere
possibilityi 
horever, certainty is not required' 
(Downev-gares v'
^Downev Conirunitv Develoornent corn. (1987) 196' cal'App'3d 983 ' 989-
,r1; Er!! ". UgIIg!{ (Gg1'tl 70 caI.APp.3d aL7, a22; In re Thorner
(19;5) I FPPC opE. rde lcopy encloseal .1 The Act seeks to prevent
^ore than actual conf I'icts of interest,' it seeks to prevent even
the appearance of a possibte conflict of interest' 
(witt v'
!,torrow, supra at 823.)
You have a fifty percent ownership interest in real property
within3oofeetoftheproposedboundariesofLakeElsinore'the
subject of rnany upcornini dlcisions by LErjlA ' It is foreseeable that
ifreie wiff be i financiit effect upon the value of your proPerty
fron decisions regarding the lake. Consequently you nay not
participate in any of those decisions if the effect on your
property is material.
Materia I itv
Regulation L8702.1(a) (3), coPy enclosed, states that the
effect of i govern:nental decision on an interest in real property
aiiectly inv5lved in a decision before an official's 
agency is
rnaterial when:
(A) The decision involves the zoning or
rezoning, annexation or deannexation, sa1e,
purchase, or lease, or incLusion in or exclusion
frorn any city, county, district 
or other local
governmental subdivision, of real property in which
the official 
has a direct or indirect interest
(other than a leasehoLd interest) of 51,000 or more,
or a sinilar decision affecting such property;


.our Fil.e No. I-9O-77 4
Page 4
(B) The decision invoLves the issuance, denial
or revocation of a license, permit or other land use
entitlement authorizing a specific use or uses of
such property;
(c) The decision involves the imposition,
repeal or modification of any taxes or fees assessed
or imposed on such proPerty; or
(D) The decision is to designate the survey
area, to select the proiect area, to adopt the
prelininary plan, to forn a project area cornmittee,
Lo certify the environnental docunent, to adopt the
redeveloprnent p1an, to add territory to the
redevelopment area, or to rescind or anend any of
the abovL decisions,' and real property in which the
official 
has an interest, or any part of it is
located within the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the redeveloprnent area.
(E) For purposes of this subdivision, the
terms'trzoningrr and "rezoning" shal'I refer to the act
of establishing or changing the zoning or land use
designation on the subject ProPerty, but shaII not
refer to an anendment of an existi.ng zoning
ordinance or other land use regulation (such as
changes in the uses pernitted, or development 
-
staniards applicable, within a particular zoning
category) which is applicable to aII other
properties designated in that category.
Thus the effect on reaL property subject to decisions as
listed above sould be Daterial.
uateriality 
is gauged sonerrhat differently in connection with
real property interests that are indirectlv affected by
governmental decisions.
Regul-ation La702.3 (copy enclosed) deals with interests in
real pr6perty indirectlv affected by governmental decj'sions and
sets forth the following, in pertinent part:
(a) The effect of a decision is naterial as
to real property in which an official 
has a direct,
indirect, oi beneficial ovnership interest. 
-, if
any of the following applies.
(1) The real property in which the official
has an interest, or any part of that real
property, is located uithin a 3oo foot radius
of the boundaries or the proposed boundaries
of the property which is the subject of the
decision, unless the decision wiII have no


.Our File No. I-9O-77 4
Page 5
financial effect upon the official's 
real
property interest.
(2') The decision involves construction of, or
improvements to, streets, hrater, sewer, storm
drainage or similar faciJ.ities, and the real
property in which the official 
has an interest
will receive new or substantially inproved
services.
Regulation 18702.3 (a) .
since your lakefront property is within 300 feet of the
proposed boundary of Lake Elsinore, under Regulation
La7o2.3(a) (1), any LEUA decision regarding the water leve1s of the
lake would be material unless it resulted in no financial effect
upon you or your property. Subdivision (a) (2) dictates that a
decision will be naterial if it results in irnproved services to
your property. Thus, if there is any way that a LEMA decision
resuLts in better sewer or storm drainage or street, walkway, or
sirnilar improvements, that circumstance would also resuLt in a
deterrnination that the effect of the decision was material. 
(See
Freidin advice letter, No. A-89-214; copy enclosed. )
PubIic ceneraLlv
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is material, disqualification is required only if the
effect of the decision is distinguishable fron the effect of the
decision upon the public generally. In your case, the question
would be lrhether lakefront property owners are a significant
segment of the public residing or doing business within the
jurisdiction of the seven local agencies which are the described
in submitted rnaterials as the principal agencies which comprise
LEltA. Jurisdicti.on is def ined as follows:
"Jurisdictionrr neans the state with respect to
a state agency and, with respect to a local
governnent agency, the region, county, city,
district 
or other geographical area in which it has
jurisdiction. 
The jurisdi-ction of a menber of a
regional coastal zone conservation cornnission shall
be the pernit area in which the regional comnission
has jurisdiction. 
Real property shall be deemed to
be 'iwithin the jurisdj,ction" with respect to a
Iocal government agency if the property or any part
of it is located within or not more than two niles
outside the boundaries of the jurisdiction or
within two miles of any Iand owned or used by the
local government agency.
section 82035.


Our File No. l-9O-77 4
Page 6
In your case, the relevant jurisdiction would be two niles
beyond the boundaries of the seven agencies which comprise LExrtA.
From the nap subrnitted, it appears that the lakefront property
owners do not form a significant segment of the public in the
jurisdictional 
area. (See cosqrove advice }etter, No. A-89-12o;
copy enclosed) . 
Even if the lakefront property olrners did form a
significant segrnent of the public, the fact that you have a
financial interest in rnore than one lakefront property neans that
for the irpublic general.ly'r exception to apply to you, a
significant segrent of the public of the jurisdj.ction would have
to have a sirnilar ownership interest. 
since we have been given no
indication that such is the case, we assume that your ownership
interest in lakefront properties is distinguishable fron that of
the public generally.
Taking alI of the above into consideration, it appears that
decisions on the lake $/ater leveI would have a reasonably
foreseeable rnaterial financial effect upon your property,
distingui-shable from the effect upon the public AeneraIIy, and
that, therefore, you must disqualify yoursel-f from participation
in such decisions.
You have also requested advice lrith respect to whether you
nay participate in LEHA decisions to acquire parcels of land
sirnilar to your own. The analysis here is somewhat conplex.
Regulation J-8702.3 (a) (1) would require your disqua 1i f ication if
the boundaries of any of the properties to be acquired are within
3oo feet of your property, unless there is no financial inpact
upon you or your property fron the decision. Bear in rnind that
financial effect need not be beneficial. 
Any financial effect,
beneficial or detrinental lrould reguire disqua I if ication. 
(See
Phelps advice Letter, No. A-88-429; copy enclosed. )
Additionally, if services to your property are inproved by
the decision, under Regulation 18702.3(a) (2), you lrould have to
disqualify yourself. 
Please see discussion above in connection
with your first question.
ff the acquisition decision involves property located between
300 and 2500 feet of the boundaries of your property, then the
relevant authority is Regulation 18702.3(a) (3) which sets forth
the following:
(a) The effect of a decision is material as
to real property in dhich an official 
has a direct,
indirect or beneficial ownership interest (not
including a Leasehold interest), if any of the
following applies:
***
(3) The real property in which the
official 
has an interest is located outside a
radius of 300 feet and any part of the real


Our Fi■e No.  
■-90-774
Page 7
property is located r.rithin a radius of 2,5oo
feet of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property srhich is the
subject of the decision and the decision wilI
have a reasonabl.y foreseeable financial effect
of :
(A) Ten thousand dollars (S10,000)
or nore on the fair market value of the
real property in which the official 
has
an interest; or
(B) wiII affect the rental value of
the property by S1,000 or more per 12
nonth period.
Regutation 18702.3 (a) (3).
Thus, under this regulation, you would have to disqualify
yourself from any acquisition decision that could foreseeably
increase or decrease the fair narket value of any of your
properties by S10,000 or more or the rental value of any of your
oroperties by Sr,ooo or more in a twelve nonth Period, unless the
"public generall.y'r exception $rould appIy. (See wiczvnski advice
Ietter, A-9o-162, copy enclosed. ) For the reasons discussed
rbove, in the section concerning the t'public generally" exception,
we do not bel,ieve that that exception vould be pertinent.
It is up to you to nake a reasonable determination r,rhether
any acquisition decision regarding property located fron 300 to
2,500 feet of your property will effect the fair narket vaLue or
rental value sufficiently to be material under the standards set
forth in Regulation L8702.3 (a) (3). The FPPC cannot determine
vaLuation for you. However, Regulation La702.3(d) does provide
factors which must be considered together with any other relevant
facts i.n rnaking your determination. Those factors which nust be
considered in rnaking your determination are:
(d) For a decision which is covered by
subdivision (a) (3) or (b) (1) or (c), factors which
shall be considered j,n deternining whether the
decision wiLl have the effects set forth in
subdivision (a) (3) (A) or (B) include, but are not
linited to:
(1) The proximity of the property which
is the subject of the decision and the
nagnitude of the proposed project or change in
use in relationship to the property j.n which
the official 
has an interest;
(2) whether it j,s reasonably foreseeable
that the decision wiIl affect the development
potential or incone producing potential of the
property ;


(3) In addition to the foregoing, in r-he
case of residential- proPerty, hrhether it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision wiLl
result in a change to the character of the
neighborhood including, but not limited to,
effects on traffic, 
view, privacy, intensity
of use, noise levels, air e:nissions, or
similar traits of the neighborhood-
Regulation L8702.3 (d) , enphasis added.
The above list is not intended to be all-inclusive 
and there
may be other relevant considerations to take into account'
cenerally, decisions to acquire property more than 2,5o0 feet
fron the boundaries of your property will not be deemed to have a
rnaterial financial errect in your property. However, if there are
specific circumstances which make it reasonably foreseeable that
such a decision will affect the market value of your property by
Slo,ooo or the rental value by 51,ooo, the effect rnay be considered
miteriaf depending upon your the circurnstances of other properties
located witirin zsoo ieet- of your property. 
(See Regulation
L87 02 .3 (b) . )
You subnj.tted a third question regarding resident use
but subsequentl.y withdrew that question in a telephone
conversation. we therefore have not included any response
question.

since we have been given no
indication that such is the case, we assume that your ownership
interest in lakefront properties is distinguishable fron that of
the public generally.
Taking alI of the above into consideration, it appears that
decisions on the lake $/ater leveI would have a reasonably
foreseeable rnaterial financial effect upon your property,
distingui-shable from the effect upon the public AeneraIIy, and
that, therefore, you must disqualify yoursel-f from participation
in such decisions.
You have also requested advice lrith respect to whether you
nay participate in LEHA decisions to acquire parcels of land
sirnilar to your own. The analysis here is somewhat conplex.
Regulation J-8702.3 (a) (1) would require your disqua 1i f ication if
the boundaries of any of the properties to be acquired are within
3oo feet of your property, unless there is no financial inpact
upon you or your property fron the decision. Bear in rnind that
financial effect need not be beneficial. 
Any financial effect,
beneficial or detrinental lrould reguire disqua I if ication. 
(See
Phelps advice Letter, No. A-88-429; copy enclosed. )
Additionally, if services to your property are inproved by
the decision, under Regulation 18702.3(a) (2), you lrould have to
disqualify yourself. 
Please see discussion above in connection
with your first question.
ff the acquisition decision involves property located between
300 and 2500 feet of the boundaries of your property, then the
relevant authority is Regulation 18702.3(a) (3) which sets forth
the following:
(a) The effect of a decision is material as
to real property in dhich an official 
has a direct,
indirect or beneficial ownership interest (not
